text: The surgeon creates pneumoperitoneum and flips the greater omentum and transverse colon to identify the middle colic vessels. The transverse colon is dissected to expose the middle colic artery, which is clipped and cut. Further dissection reveals the gastrocolic trunk of Henle entering the superior mesenteric vein from the right side. The gastric branch of the gastrocolic trunk is clipped and cut, exposing the middle colic vein, which is also clipped and cut. The right gastroepiploic artery is exposed and preserved, while the colic branch of the gastrocolic trunk is clipped and cut.

The surgeon performs further dissection near the duodenal jejunal flexure to expose the inferior mesenteric vein, which is then divided at its origin. The preserved gastroepiploic artery and the inferior border of the pancreas are clearly visible. Additional dissection releases the duodenal jejunal flexure. The transverse colon is mobilized by taking down both the hepatic and splenic flexures. The gastrocolic ligament is divided to expose and take down the hepatic flexure, and the Cattell-Braasch maneuver is used to expose the duodenum.

The surgeon mobilizes the entire right hemicolon by dissecting along the white line of Toldt. The remaining gastrocolic ligament is divided from the transverse mesocolon towards the left side, entering the lesser sac to visualize the posterior surface of the stomach. The dissection continues towards the left side of the colon, performing the Mattox maneuver to take down the splenic flexure. The dissection proceeds down the lateral aspect of the descending colon's white line of Toldt. After completing the transverse colon mobilization, extracorporeal resection anastomosis is performed using a sergi sleeve.
The surgical specimen of the transverse colon is displayed, featuring India ink tattooing that marks the location of the stenosing tumor.
